content.problemStatement:
Chef is given two strings AAA and BBB of length NNN containing lowercase English letters.

Chef can rearrange both the strings in any way he wants. He wants to minimize the length of LCS (Longest Common Subsequence) of both the strings.

Find the minimum length of LCS of AAA and BBB if he optimally rearranges both the strings.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - denoting the length of AAA and BBB.
The second line of each test case contains a string AAA.
The third line of each test case contains a string BBB.




Output Format
For each test case, output the minimum length of LCS Chef can obtain.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
A and B contain only lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N, the length of the two strings. The second line contains string A of length N. The third line contains string B of length N. All strings consist of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum possible length of the longest common subsequence after arbitrarily permuting both strings.

sampleTestCases.input:
3
4
aaaa
aaab
1
c
z
5
abcde
cdefg
sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: character 'a' appears 4 times in A and 3 times in B, min=3, all other mins are 0, max=3. Test case 2: no common character, answer 0. Test case 3: mins are 1 for c, d, e, maximum is 1.

First case strings are identical, each character appears once in both, max min =1 for each, but all three characters can be matched simultaneously, giving LCS length 3. Second case we can order A as abc and B as cba; the longest common subsequence is any single character, so answer is 1.
Frequencies: a:2 vs2 ->2, b:3 vs2 ->2, c:0 vs2 ->0. Maximum of mins is 2, which is achievable by arranging blocks oppositely.

solution.approach: For any character c, after rearranging the strings we can always take all occurrences of c that appear in both strings as a common subsequence, giving a subsequence of length min(cntA[c], cntB[c]). Therefore the LCS length can never be smaller than the maximum of these values over all characters. By arranging the blocks of each character in opposite orders in the two strings we can ensure that no longer mixed‑character subsequence exists, so the lower bound is achievable. Hence the answer is max_c min(cntA[c], cntB[c]). The algorithm counts frequencies of each letter in both strings and computes this maximum.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the strings.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only two arrays of size 26 are used.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        A = next(it)
        B = next(it)
        cntA = [0]*26
        cntB = [0]*26
        for ch in A:
            cntA[ord(ch)-97] += 1
        for ch in B:
            cntB[ord(ch)-97] += 1
        ans = 0
        for i in range(26):
            ans = max(ans, min(cntA[i], cntB[i]))
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
